title: 40 Years of Terror Ended by a DNA Website
description:
The Golden State Killer — For over a decade, a masked predator terrorized California, committing at least 13 murders, 50 sexual assaults, and 120 burglaries across multiple jurisdictions. Known by different names in different regions, he evaded capture for over 40 years until a revolutionary use of consumer DNA databases led to his identification in 2018 as former police officer Joseph James DeAngelo.

Sources: Sacramento County District Attorney case files, FBI VICAP records, Orange County Superior Court transcripts, California Department of Justice investigation records.

DISCLAIMER: This video is for educational and documentary purposes only. All information presented is sourced from publicly available records, news reports, and official statements. Viewer discretion is advised.

#truecrime #goldenstatekiller #DNA #coldcase #solved #serialkiller #california #forensics #justice #darkfiles
sources: Sacramento County District Attorney case files
FBI Violent Criminal Apprehension Program (VICAP) records

segments.voice: For over forty years, a phantom haunted the state of California. He moved through the darkness like a predator, entering homes through unlocked windows, prowling suburban neighborhoods with impunity. He committed at least thirteen murders, fifty sexual assaults, and over one hundred and twenty burglaries across a decade-long crime spree that stretched from Sacramento to Southern California. He was known by many names: the Visalia Ransacker, the East Area Rapist, the Original Night Stalker. But eventually, he would be known by one name that encompassed all his crimes: the Golden State Killer.
segments.visual: suburban neighborhood street at night dark houses

segments.voice: The crimes began in the mid-1970s in Visalia, a small city in California's Central Valley. According to Visalia Police Department records, a serial burglar broke into over a hundred homes between 1974 and 1975, ransacking belongings and stealing personal items, often women's undergarments. The crimes escalated when the burglar, dubbed the Visalia Ransacker, shot and killed a college journalism professor named Claude Snelling on September 11, 1975. Snelling had confronted the intruder as he attempted to kidnap his daughter from the family home. The killer fled into the night.
segments.visual: quiet suburban house at night with porch light

segments.voice: In 1976, the crimes shifted north to Sacramento, where a new pattern of attacks began that would terrorize the region for nearly three years. According to Sacramento County Sheriff's Department records, a masked attacker began breaking into homes and sexually assaulting women, often binding their partners or family members in adjacent rooms. He was methodical and prepared, having clearly surveilled his victims beforehand. He would call victims before and after attacks, whispering threats. He became known as the East Area Rapist, and he attacked at least fifty victims in the Sacramento area.
segments.visual: dark window at night from outside house

segments.voice: According to police reports and victim statements, the East Area Rapist displayed a terrifying level of preparation. He would enter homes carrying pre-cut lengths of rope, and he would stack dishes on the backs of bound victims, telling them he would hear the dishes crash if they moved and would kill them. He stole personal items from homes weeks before attacks, learning layouts and routines. Some victims reported that their dogs had been drugged or that their gates had been unlocked days before the assault. He was a planner of exceptional patience and cruelty.
segments.visual: rope and tools on dark surface

segments.voice: Despite a massive law enforcement response, the East Area Rapist remained unidentified. According to Sacramento County records, hundreds of officers were assigned to the case, task forces were formed, and community meetings drew thousands of frightened residents. DNA profiling did not yet exist, and the physical evidence recovered from crime scenes, including biological evidence, could not be matched to a suspect using the technology of the 1970s. The attacker seemed to anticipate police tactics, altering his patterns and striking in new areas just as investigators focused on specific neighborhoods.
segments.visual: community meeting hall crowded with people

segments.voice: In 1979, the attacks in Sacramento suddenly stopped. But according to law enforcement records, crimes matching the same pattern began occurring in Southern California, starting in Santa Barbara County and eventually moving to Orange County and Ventura County. The attacks now included murder. Between 1979 and 1986, a killer known as the Original Night Stalker murdered at least twelve people in Southern California, most of them couples in their homes. For years, investigators did not connect these murders to the East Area Rapist attacks four hundred miles to the north.
segments.visual: california highway at sunset driving south

segments.voice: The connection between the East Area Rapist and the Original Night Stalker was not established until 2001, when DNA evidence from the Sacramento sexual assaults was matched to DNA from the Southern California murders. According to the California Department of Justice, this linkage was a breakthrough, revealing that a single individual had conducted a crime spree spanning over a decade and hundreds of miles. The combined criminal was given a new designation by journalist Michelle McNamara in a 2013 article for Los Angeles Magazine: the Golden State Killer.
segments.visual: forensic DNA analysis laboratory equipment

segments.voice: Michelle McNamara, a true crime writer married to comedian Patton Oswalt, became obsessed with solving the case. According to her research, later published posthumously in her book 'I'll Be Gone in the Dark,' McNamara spent years analyzing police reports, interviewing victims and investigators, and exploring leads that law enforcement had not pursued. She was convinced the killer was hiding in plain sight, living an ordinary life in a California suburb. Tragically, McNamara died in her sleep in April 2016 at age forty-six, before the killer was identified.
segments.visual: writer working late night desk lamp research

segments.voice: The breakthrough that finally solved the case came not from traditional police work but from a revolutionary application of consumer DNA technology. According to the Sacramento County District Attorney's office, investigator Paul Holes and attorney Anne Marie Schubert developed a strategy to upload crime scene DNA to GEDmatch, a free genealogy database used by hobbyists to find relatives. The DNA from the Golden State Killer's crimes was processed and uploaded to the platform in early 2018. The results identified distant relatives of the unknown killer, setting off a genetic manhunt.
segments.visual: genealogy family tree diagram on screen

segments.voice: According to court documents, genetic genealogist Barbara Rae-Venter worked with investigators to build a family tree from the GEDmatch results. By tracing distant familial DNA matches through public genealogy records, birth certificates, and census data, the team narrowed the suspect pool from millions of Californians to a handful of individuals. One name stood out: Joseph James DeAngelo Jr., a seventy-two-year-old man living in Citrus Heights, a quiet Sacramento suburb. He was a retired mechanic. He had no criminal record. And he was a former police officer.
segments.visual: suburban house with garden ordinary neighborhood

segments.voice: The revelation that the suspect was a former law enforcement officer sent shockwaves through the investigation. According to employment records obtained by investigators, Joseph DeAngelo had served as a police officer in Exeter, California, from 1973 to 1976, and then in Auburn, California, from 1976 to 1979. He was fired from the Auburn Police Department in 1979 after being caught shoplifting dog repellent and a hammer. His time as a police officer overlapped precisely with the early years of the crime spree, explaining his intimate knowledge of police procedures and investigation tactics.
segments.visual: police badge and uniform close up

segments.voice: Before making an arrest, investigators needed to confirm the DNA match. According to the arrest warrant affidavit, undercover officers surveilled DeAngelo's home and collected discarded items containing his DNA. One account describes agents recovering a tissue from his trash. The DNA from these items was compared to the crime scene DNA preserved for decades. According to the forensic lab results, the match was conclusive. Joseph James DeAngelo was the Golden State Killer. On April 24, 2018, he was arrested in his driveway. He appeared stunned, reportedly mumbling about a roast in the oven.
segments.visual: evidence collection forensic gloves

segments.voice: The arrest of Joseph DeAngelo was announced at a press conference that drew national attention. According to Sacramento County District Attorney Anne Marie Schubert, the identification represented a paradigm shift in criminal investigation. For the first time, consumer DNA databases had been used to identify a serial criminal. Schubert stated that the case proved that violent offenders could no longer hide behind the passage of time. She directly addressed the victims, telling them their courage and perseverance had made this day possible. Many victims watched the press conference in tears.
segments.visual: press conference room full of reporters cameras

segments.voice: DeAngelo's double life was perhaps the most disturbing aspect of the case. According to neighbors, coworkers, and acquaintances interviewed after his arrest, he had lived an unremarkable suburban existence for decades. He had married, raised a family, worked as a mechanic at a Save Mart distribution center, and maintained a modest home with a fishing boat in the driveway. His neighbors described him as quiet and sometimes irritable but otherwise ordinary. None of them had any idea they had been living next to one of the most prolific serial criminals in American history.
segments.visual: ordinary suburban house with lawn daytime

segments.voice: According to court documents filed in the case, the charges against DeAngelo were staggering in scope. He was charged with thirteen counts of first-degree murder with special circumstances, including multiple murder and murder during the commission of burglary and sexual assault. The charges spanned six counties in California. Because some of the crimes occurred before 1978, when California's current death penalty statute took effect, and because of the statute of limitations on the sexual assault charges, prosecutors could only charge the murders. But the full scope of his crimes was presented to the court.
segments.visual: courtroom interior solemn legal proceedings

segments.voice: The victims and their families had waited decades for this moment. According to victim impact statements filed with the court, survivors described lives shattered by the attacks. Many had suffered from severe post-traumatic stress, anxiety, and depression for forty years. Some had been unable to form intimate relationships. Others lived in constant fear, checking locks and windows obsessively. One victim described how the attack had robbed her of the ability to feel safe in her own home, a violation that no amount of time could heal. Their testimony was devastating and powerful.
segments.visual: woman looking through window blinds anxious

segments.voice: In June 2020, Joseph James DeAngelo pleaded guilty to thirteen counts of murder and admitted to the sexual assaults under a plea agreement that took the death penalty off the table. According to the plea transcript, DeAngelo, now seventy-four years old and appearing frail in a wheelchair, answered the judge's questions in a barely audible whisper. When asked to confirm each count, he replied with a quiet 'I admit.' It was the first time the Golden State Killer had ever acknowledged his crimes. The courtroom was silent except for the quiet weeping of survivors.
segments.visual: wheelchair in empty hallway institutional

segments.voice: The sentencing hearing in August 2020 was one of the most emotional courtroom events in California history. According to court transcripts, survivors and family members delivered victim impact statements over four days. They spoke directly to DeAngelo, describing the terror he had inflicted and the decades of suffering that followed. One victim told him he had stolen her ability to trust. Another described thirty years of nightmares. A murder victim's daughter told DeAngelo he would die in prison knowing he had destroyed her family. DeAngelo sat motionless and expressionless throughout.
segments.visual: microphone at witness stand courtroom

segments.voice: Joseph James DeAngelo was sentenced to multiple consecutive terms of life in prison without the possibility of parole. According to the sentencing order, he will die in prison. The judge called his crimes unspeakable and stated that the sentence reflected the magnitude of suffering he had caused over more than a decade of predatory violence. DeAngelo was transferred to a state prison facility, where he is expected to spend the remainder of his life. He has not spoken publicly since his guilty plea and has offered no explanation for his crimes.
segments.visual: prison corridor long hallway locked doors

segments.voice: The use of genetic genealogy to identify DeAngelo opened a new era in criminal investigation but also ignited a fierce debate about privacy. According to civil liberties organizations, the technique raises profound questions about the use of consumer DNA data. When individuals submit their DNA to genealogy websites, they may not anticipate that their genetic information could be used by law enforcement to identify relatives involved in crimes. The GEDmatch database subsequently changed its terms of service to require users to opt in to law enforcement searches.
segments.visual: DNA double helix model scientific

segments.voice: Investigator Paul Holes, who had worked the Golden State Killer case for over twenty years, described the moment of identification as the culmination of a lifelong quest. According to his published interviews, Holes had spent countless hours reviewing evidence, interviewing survivors, and chasing leads that went nowhere. He retired from the Contra Costa County District Attorney's office just days before DeAngelo's arrest, after dedicating the final years of his career to the case. The identification came not through a single eureka moment but through the patient accumulation of decades of work.
segments.visual: detective at desk reviewing evidence files

segments.voice: Michelle McNamara's posthumous book 'I'll Be Gone in the Dark' was published in February 2018, just two months before DeAngelo's arrest. According to publishing records, the book became a bestseller and was later adapted into an HBO documentary series. In the book's epilogue, McNamara addressed the unknown killer directly, writing prophetically that a day of reckoning was coming. Her dedication to the case and her faith that the killer would be found proved tragically prescient. She did not live to see justice, but her work helped ensure it arrived.
segments.visual: book on table with reading light

segments.voice: Since the DeAngelo case, genetic genealogy has been used to identify suspects in dozens of cold cases across the United States. According to the FBI, the technique has become one of the most powerful tools in modern criminal investigation. Cases that had been cold for decades, with no suspects and no leads, have been solved using the same methodology that identified the Golden State Killer. The technology has also been used to identify unidentified remains, giving names back to victims who had been anonymous for years. A new frontier in forensic science had been opened.
segments.visual: computer screen with genetic data analysis

segments.voice: According to law enforcement records, DeAngelo's career as a police officer gave him extraordinary advantages in evading detection. He understood evidence collection, witness procedures, and investigation tactics. He knew which forensic techniques were available and which were not. He used gloves to avoid fingerprints, wore masks to prevent identification, and selected victims in patterns designed to confuse investigators trying to establish geographic profiles. His law enforcement training was weaponized against the very system he had sworn to serve and protect.
segments.visual: latex gloves and forensic kit close up

segments.voice: The psychological profile of the Golden State Killer has been studied extensively by criminal behaviorists. According to FBI profilers who examined the case, DeAngelo displayed characteristics of a power-reassurance offender who derived satisfaction from domination and control. His habit of calling victims before and after attacks, his meticulous planning, and his theatrical presentation during crimes all pointed to someone who needed to feel omnipotent. His career in law enforcement, which is itself a position of authority and control, may have been part of the same psychological pattern.
segments.visual: shadow figure behind frosted glass dark

segments.voice: For the communities that lived through the terror, DeAngelo's arrest brought a complex mixture of relief and renewed pain. According to interviews with Sacramento-area residents who remembered the East Area Rapist attacks, the identification validated decades of fear and vigilance. Some residents had never stopped looking over their shoulders, never stopped checking their locks twice before bed. The arrest confirmed that the monster had been real, that their fear had been justified. But it also meant confronting the reality that the monster had been living among them all along.
segments.visual: neighborhood watch sign on residential street

segments.voice: The case of the Golden State Killer stands as both a triumph and a tragedy. According to criminal justice scholars, it is a triumph of perseverance, of technology, and of the dedication of investigators who refused to let the case die. But it is also a tragedy of immense proportions: at least thirteen lives lost, fifty people sexually assaulted, hundreds of homes invaded, and thousands of lives altered by fear. Joseph DeAngelo committed his crimes over more than a decade and hid for more than four decades. Justice arrived, but for many, it arrived far too late.
segments.visual: sunrise over quiet suburban neighborhood

segments.voice: Today, Joseph James DeAngelo sits in a California state prison, serving multiple life sentences without the possibility of parole. According to prison records, he is housed in a single cell and has no contact with general population inmates. The man who terrorized a state for over a decade will spend his remaining years behind walls and bars. And somewhere, in a genealogy database, the DNA that finally identified him sits as a permanent record, a digital witness to the truth that time and technology eventually catch up with even the most careful criminals. The Golden State Killer's reign is over. Forever.
segments.visual: prison exterior high walls and fences
